auth_type: Token-based via Web API or NTLM
auth_notes: Requires the SolidWorks PDM Web API (Web2) to be installed and configured on an IIS server. Authentication typically involves a POST request to a login endpoint to retrieve a session cookie or token, which must be passed in subsequent headers. If using Windows Auth, NTLM configuration in the HTTP Request node is required.
rate_limits: No fixed public limits; restricted by host IIS server resources and SQL Server performance.
gotchas: The standard PDM desktop client has no REST API; the Web API/Web2 component must be explicitly installed and licensed.
File operations (Check-in/Check-out) via REST are highly sensitive to multi-part form data formatting and local vault view constraints.
Webhooks are not native; they must be custom-coded as PDM 'Add-ins' or 'Dispatch' scripts that trigger HTTP requests to n8n upon vault events.
client_must_provide: VPN or Firewall access for n8n to reach the internal IIS server
research_notes: Integration must use the HTTP Request node. Because PDM is almost always on-premise, the n8n instance must have network line-of-sight to the PDM Web API server. The workflow must handle session persistence (cookies) and binary data for file transfers. For real-time triggers, a custom C# Add-in must be written in the PDM Administration tool to call n8n webhooks.

effort_recommendation.rationale: Integration must use the HTTP Request node. Because PDM is almost always on-premise, the n8n instance must have network line-of-sight to the PDM Web API server. The workflow must handle session persistence (cookies) and binary data for file transfers. For real-time triggers, a custom C# Add-in must be written in the PDM Administration tool to call n8n webhooks.
